1: Sing, O barren one who has not borne; break out into singing and cry aloud, you who have not been in labor! For the children of the desolate one will be more than the children of the married woman, says YHWH. This verse opens Isaiah 54 as the direct continuation of the Servant's self-offering in Isaiah 53: the barren woman (desolate Zion) who lost her children to exile now sings because the Servant's atoning work has opened the womb of the covenant community. Paul quotes this verse directly in Galatians 4:27 as the oracle of the Jerusalem above — she is the mother of us all. He applies it to the contrast between Hagar (the slave, earthly Jerusalem under the law) and Sarah (the free woman, the new covenant community). The barren woman who has more children than the married woman is the community of the Servant — the church — whose children come through the new birth of the Spirit, not natural generation. The impossible fertility promised to the barren is the pattern of the gospel's expansion: the community that seemed most depleted (post-cross, post-exile) produces the most children.
2: Enlarge the place of your tent, and let the curtains of your habitations be stretched out; do not hold back; lengthen your cords and strengthen your stakes. The expand-your-tent command is the logistical consequence of v 1's unexpected fertility: the community must prepare for an influx it did not anticipate. The tent imagery is both nomadic (wilderness-covenant) and the tabernacle (YHWH's dwelling among his people). The stretching of cords and strengthening of stakes is the infrastructure of a growing community. In the NT: Pentecost (Acts 2:41 — three thousand added in one day) and the subsequent Gentile mission (Acts 13-28) are the historical fulfillment of the tent-expansion command. The stakes-and-cords work of Paul's missionary journeys is the literal stretching of the tent across the Mediterranean world.
3: For you will spread out to the right and to the left; your offspring will possess the nations and will people the desolate cities. The right-and-left expansion is the geographic promise: Zion will inherit what was empty. The desolate cities — the ruins of judgment oracles throughout Isaiah — will be repopulated by the children of the barren one. In the NT: the Great Commission (Matthew 28:18-20: go and make disciples of all nations) is the Servant-community's possession of the nations. The desolate cities of the Gentile world are repeopled by the church's mission. Paul's calling to proclaim the gospel where Christ was not known (Romans 15:20) is the right-and-left spread of the expanded tent.
4: Do not fear, for you will not be ashamed; do not be humiliated, for you will not be disgraced. For you will forget the shame of your youth, and the reproach of your widowhood you will remember no more. The shame-removal promise follows the three do-not-fears of Second Isaiah (Isaiah 41:10, Isaiah 43:1, Isaiah 44:2) and echoes the cornerstone promise of Isaiah 28:16 (whoever trusts in it will not be put to shame). The shame-of-your-youth (Egypt's slavery) and the reproach-of-widowhood (the exile) are the two great traumas whose memory is erased by YHWH's restoration. In the NT: there is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus (Romans 8:1) — the shame and reproach of sin's verdict is removed by the Servant who bore it in Isaiah 53. Those who trust in the cornerstone will not be put to shame (Romans 10:11).
5: For your Maker is your husband — YHWH of hosts is his name — and the Holy One of Israel is your Redeemer; he is called the God of all the earth. The Maker-as-husband metaphor is the covenant relationship expressed as marriage: YHWH is not merely Zion's protector but her closest kin. In the NT, Paul develops this metaphor into the church-as-bride of Christ (Ephesians 5:25-32: husbands, love your wives as Christ loved the church and gave himself for her). The husband-metaphor applied to YHWH in Isa 54 becomes the husband-metaphor applied to Christ in Eph 5 — the NT's way of placing Christ in the position that only YHWH occupies. The God of all the earth title anticipates the universal scope of the covenant of peace (v 10): what began as Israel's covenant now encompasses all the earth.
6: For YHWH has called you like a wife deserted and grieved in spirit, like a wife of one's youth when she is rejected, says your God. The tender-rejection-and-recall metaphor is the covenant's emotional register: YHWH does not abandon in indifference but in grief, and the return is not merely legal but relational recovery. The wife of youth rejected and then recalled is the Hosea pattern (Hosea 2:14-16: I will allure her; I will lead her into the desert and speak tenderly to her). In the NT: God demonstrates his own love for us in this — while we were still sinners, Christ died for us (Romans 5:8). The recalled-wife is the sinner reconciled not by her own improvement but by the husband's initiative and cost (the Servant of Isaiah 53).
7: For a brief moment I deserted you, but with great compassion I will gather you. The brief-moment abandonment and the great-compassion restoration are asymmetric: the exile (however long it felt) is brief against the eternal compassion. The great compassion (rachamim gedolim) is the womb-love (racham — the maternal love YHWH uniquely bears). In the NT: I consider that our present sufferings are not worth comparing with the glory that will be revealed in us (Romans 8:18) — Paul applies the same asymmetry. The brief-moment darkness of the cross (My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? — Matthew 27:46) gives way to the resurrection's great-compassion gathering: the Father does not abandon the Son to the grave (Acts 2:24).
8: In a surge of wrath I hid my face from you for a moment, but with everlasting kindness I will have compassion on you, says YHWH your Redeemer. The hidden face of v 8 is the divine-abandonment language: when YHWH hides his face, the covenant community is exposed to chaos and enemy attack (Psalms 30:7: when you hid your face, I was dismayed). The surge-of-wrath moment is real — the exile, the judgment — but it is temporary against the everlasting kindness (chesed olam). In the NT: nothing in all creation will be able to separate us from the love of God that is in Christ Jesus our Lord (Romans 8:39). The everlasting-kindness guarantee of Isaiah 54:8 is the OT form of Rom 8's nothing-can-separate — both ground the eternal covenant-love against the temporary experience of absence.
9: For this is like the days of Noah to me: as I swore that the waters of Noah should no more go over the earth, so I have sworn that I will not be angry with you and will not rebuke you. The covenant with Noah (Genesis 9:11-17) is the template for the covenant of peace: just as YHWH swore never to flood the earth again, so he swears never again to abandon Zion in wrath. The rainbow-covenant becomes the type of the new covenant. In the NT: the blood of Jesus speaks a better word than the blood of Abel (Hebrews 12:24) — the new covenant's oath is sealed not by a rainbow but by the Servant's blood. The I-will-not-be-angry-with-you is the assurance that rests on the Servant's atonement of Isaiah 53 — the wrath that was poured out there will not be poured out again on those in the Servant's community.
10: For the mountains may depart and the hills be removed, but my steadfast love shall not depart from you, and my covenant of peace shall not be removed, says YHWH, who has compassion on you. The covenant of peace (berit shalom) is YHWH's guarantee that outlasts the most permanent features of creation (mountains, hills). The shalom-covenant is the new covenant that Jeremiah and Ezekiel also anticipate (Jeremiah 31:31-34, Ezekiel 37:26: I will make a covenant of peace with them — it will be an everlasting covenant). In the NT: the God of peace, who through the blood of the eternal covenant brought back from the dead our Lord Jesus, that great Shepherd of the sheep (Hebrews 13:20-21) — the covenant of shalom is the covenant sealed in the Servant's blood and ratified by the resurrection. The mountains-may-depart permanence is the NT's eternal covenant.
11: O afflicted one, storm-tossed and not comforted, behold, I will set your stones in antimony, and lay your foundations with sapphires. The storm-tossed-and-not-comforted Zion (the exile's condition) is now addressed as the recipient of jeweled reconstruction. The precious-stone foundations echo the high priest's breastplate (Exodus 28:17-21) and anticipate the New Jerusalem's foundations: the wall of the city had twelve foundations, decorated with every kind of precious stone (Revelation 21:19-20). The precious-stone city in Isa 54 is the prophetic type of the New Jerusalem in Rev 21 — the same architectural vision of YHWH's restored community expressed in cosmic materials.
12: I will make your pinnacles of rubies, your gates of crystal, and all your wall of precious stones. The four precious materials (antimony, sapphires, rubies, crystal) are continued in the gates-of-crystal and precious-stone walls. Every architectural element is precious-stone: the foundation, the pinnacles, the gates, the walls. In Revelation 21:21: the twelve gates were twelve pearls, each gate made of a single pearl; the great street of the city was of gold, as pure as transparent glass. The New Jerusalem's architecture is the eschatological fulfillment of the precious-city rebuilt here. The afflicted, storm-tossed Zion becomes the most glorious city in all creation — the pattern of the resurrection: the body sown in dishonor is raised in glory (1 Corinthians 15:43).
13: All your children will be taught by YHWH, and great will be the peace of your children. Jesus quotes this verse in John 6:45 in his discourse on the Bread of Life: It is written in the Prophets, they will all be taught by God. Everyone who listens to the Father and learns from him comes to me. The taught-by-YHWH community of Isaiah 54:13 is the community that comes to Jesus — because Jesus is where the Father's teaching leads. The great peace of the children is the fruit of YHWH's instruction: shalom is the product of being taught by the source of shalom. In the NT: the Spirit of truth who guides you into all truth (John 16:13) is the Spirit who carries out the taught-by-YHWH promise — the Paraclete as the continuing fulfillment of Isaiah 54:13 in the community of Jesus.
14: In righteousness you will be established; you will be far from oppression, for you will not fear; and from terror, for it will not come near you. The righteousness-as-foundation (established in righteousness) is the covenant community's structural stability: when the foundation is YHWH's own righteousness (cf. the cornerstone of Isaiah 28:16), oppression and terror cannot reach it. In the NT: the righteousness of God through faith in Jesus Christ for all who believe (Romans 3:22) is the foundation on which the church is established — not the community's moral achievement but the Servant's righteous act credited to those who trust in him. The far-from-terror community is the community that has been given the peace that surpasses understanding (Philippians 4:7).
15: If anyone stirs up strife, it is not from me; whoever stirs up strife with you shall fall because of you. The attack-will-fail guarantee: any who assemble against Zion do so without YHWH's authorization and will therefore fail. This is not a blanket promise against difficulty but a specific guarantee that no assault against the covenant community has divine backing. In the NT: the gates of Hades will not prevail against the church (Matthew 16:18) — the attacks on YHWH's community are real (the book of Acts, the letters to persecuted churches) but they cannot achieve their purpose of destroying what YHWH has built.
16: Behold, I have created the smith who blows the fire of coals and produces a weapon for its purpose. I have also created the ravager to destroy. YHWH is the Creator even of the weapons-maker and the destroyer: the smith who forges the weapon and the warrior who wields it are both YHWH's creations. This is the sovereignty-over-evil claim: YHWH does not merely react to human threats but governs the entire ecology of power including those who forge weapons against his community. In the NT: he works all things together for good for those who love God (Romans 8:28) — the Sovereign who created the smith who forges the weapon is the same one who uses every adversarial force for the ultimate good of his covenant community.
17: No weapon formed against you shall prosper, and every tongue that rises against you in judgment you will condemn. This is the heritage of the servants of YHWH and their vindication from me, declares YHWH. The no-weapon-shall-prosper and every-tongue-condemned are the two domains of hostile action — physical violence and verbal accusation — both of which YHWH nullifies for his servants. The heritage of the servants is the entire promise-set of this chapter: the enlarged tent, the covenant of peace, the precious-stone city, the YHWH-taught children. In the NT: who will bring any charge against those whom God has chosen? It is God who justifies. Who is the one who condemns? It is Christ Jesus who died — and who is at the right hand of God and is also interceding for us (Romans 8:33-34). The every-tongue-that-accuses is condemned by the Servant's own advocacy — the one who bore the accusations of Isaiah 53 now intercedes against every accusation brought against his servants.
